title: Water
summary: Water is where molecular structure becomes the chemistry of life. This lesson builds A1.1 from the ground up: the polar covalent bonds and bent shape that make the whole molecule polar, the hydrogen bonding this creates between neighbouring molecules, and the emergent properties that follow — cohesion and surface tension, adhesion, the thermal properties (high specific heat capacity and high latent heat of vaporisation) that buffer temperature and drive cooling, water as a near-universal solvent for hydrophilic substances, and the buoyancy and transparency that shape aquatic habitats. Each property is tied to a real biological role — transport in blood and xylem, evaporative cooling, and providing the medium for metabolism — and the model answer is laid out exactly the way our marking engine awards Paper 2 marks, so you can see how each distinct point earns its mark.
learningObjectives: Explain how polar covalent bonds and the bent shape of the molecule make water polar, and how this leads to hydrogen bonding between water molecules.
Relate hydrogen bonding to water's emergent properties: cohesion and surface tension, adhesion, thermal buffering and evaporative cooling, solvent behaviour, and buoyancy and transparency.
Describe the biological roles that follow — transport in blood and in xylem, coolant via sweating and transpiration, and water as the medium for metabolism.
Distinguish hydrophilic from hydrophobic substances and explain why polarity determines solubility.
Present an explanation so that each distinct valid point is credited the way our marking engine awards analytic marks.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: Why the shape of one small molecule runs biology
simpleExplanation.summary: Water shares its electrons unevenly and bends into a wide V, so one end is slightly negative and the other slightly positive — the molecule is polar. Polar molecules cling to each other by hydrogen bonds, and almost every remarkable thing water does for living things traces back to that clinging.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Picture each water molecule as a tiny magnet with a positive end and a negative end. One magnet's pull is feeble, but a bucket of them latch together into a surprisingly strong, self-healing web. That web is why water beads into droplets, why it resists heating and cooling, why it can be hauled to the top of a tree, and why it can wrap around salts and sugars and carry them through the body. Break the web here and it reforms there — the connections are weak one at a time but overwhelming in number.
steps: Polarity: oxygen pulls the shared electrons harder than hydrogen does, so oxygen becomes slightly negative (δ-) and each hydrogen slightly positive (δ+). The bent shape stops these charges cancelling, so the whole molecule is polar.
Hydrogen bonding: the δ+ hydrogen of one molecule is attracted to the δ- oxygen of a neighbour. Each of these attractions is weak, but there are enormous numbers of them.

Emergent properties: those hydrogen bonds give water cohesion and surface tension, adhesion, a high specific heat capacity, a high latent heat of vaporisation, solvent power for polar substances, and (as ice floats) buoyancy for aquatic life.

Biological roles: cohesion and adhesion pull water up the xylem; the solvent property makes blood plasma a transport medium; evaporation cools the body through sweating and cools leaves through transpiration; and water is the medium in which metabolism happens.

flashcards.back: A covalent bond in which electrons are shared UNEQUALLY. Oxygen is more electronegative than hydrogen, so it pulls the shared electrons closer, gaining a partial negative charge (δ-) and leaving each hydrogen partially positive (δ+).

flashcards.front: Why the whole water molecule is polar
flashcards.back: The molecule is BENT (about 105°), so the two O–H dipoles do not cancel. The oxygen end is δ- and the hydrogen end is δ+, giving the molecule an overall separation of charge — it is polar.
A weak INTERMOLECULAR attraction between the δ+ hydrogen of one polar molecule and a δ- atom (here oxygen) of a neighbouring molecule. Weak individually, strong collectively.

Hydrogen bonds form BETWEEN water molecules (intermolecular). The O–H covalent bonds are WITHIN a molecule (intramolecular) and are far stronger. A common exam error is to place hydrogen bonds inside the molecule.
Attraction between molecules of the SAME substance. Water molecules hydrogen-bond to one another, giving cohesion — this holds the water column in xylem together and produces surface tension.
Attraction between molecules of DIFFERENT substances. Water hydrogen-bonds to other polar surfaces, e.g. the cellulose walls of xylem, helping water rise and cling.

A large amount of energy is needed to raise water's temperature because energy first goes into breaking hydrogen bonds, not into molecular kinetic energy. This BUFFERS temperature for cells and for aquatic habitats.

A large amount of energy is needed to evaporate water because many hydrogen bonds must be broken to free molecules as vapour. Evaporation therefore removes a lot of heat — the basis of cooling by sweating and by transpiration.

Because it is polar, water forms hydration shells around ions and polar (hydrophilic) molecules, dissolving them. It cannot dissolve non-polar (hydrophobic) substances such as lipids — this too is biologically vital (membranes).
HYDROPHILIC (‘water-loving’): polar or charged, dissolves in / interacts with water (e.g. glucose, ions). HYDROPHOBIC (‘water-fearing’): non-polar, does not dissolve (e.g. fats, oils).

Ice is less dense than liquid water (open hydrogen-bonded lattice), so it floats and insulates the water below — organisms survive under it. Water is transparent, letting light reach aquatic plants and algae for photosynthesis.

flashcards.front: Exam Trap: are hydrogen bonds stronger than covalent bonds?
flashcards.back: No. A single hydrogen bond is far WEAKER than the O–H covalent bond. Water's properties come from the sheer NUMBER of hydrogen bonds acting together, not from any one being strong.

flashcards.front: Exam Trap: ‘water is important for life’
flashcards.back: A property claim with no MECHANISM scores nothing. You must link structure → property → biological use, e.g. polar → hydrogen bonds → cohesion → water pulled up xylem.

quickCheck.prompt: Where are the hydrogen bonds in a sample of liquid water located?
options: Within each molecule, between the O and H atoms
Between neighbouring water molecules, from a δ+ hydrogen to a δ- oxygen
Between the two hydrogen atoms of the same molecule
Only in ice, not in liquid water
quickCheck.answer: Between neighbouring water molecules, from a δ+ hydrogen to a δ- oxygen

quickCheck.prompt: Water rises up the narrow xylem vessels of a plant partly because it sticks to the cellulose walls. This sticking of water to a different substance is best described as:
options: Cohesion
Adhesion
Surface tension
Capillary repulsion

quickCheck.prompt: Why does water's high specific heat capacity help living organisms?
options: It lets water heat up very quickly to warm cells
Energy is stored as chemical bonds inside each molecule
Large heat gains or losses cause only small temperature changes, buffering internal and habitat temperatures
It allows water to dissolve non-polar substances
quickCheck.answer: Large heat gains or losses cause only small temperature changes, buffering internal and habitat temperatures

quickCheck.prompt: Which pair of substances would BOTH dissolve readily in water?
options: A lipid and a wax
Sodium chloride (ionic) and glucose (polar)
Oil and cholesterol
Nitrogen gas and a phospholipid tail

quickCheck.prompt: Sweating cools the body because water has a high latent heat of vaporisation. This means that when sweat evaporates:
options: It releases heat into the skin, warming it
It takes a large amount of heat energy from the body to break hydrogen bonds and turn liquid into vapour
The salt in sweat reacts with the skin to absorb heat
Water molecules gain kinetic energy from the air and warm the body
quickCheck.answer: It takes a large amount of heat energy from the body to break hydrogen bonds and turn liquid into vapour

faq.q: Are the hydrogen bonds inside a water molecule or between water molecules?
faq.a: Between. The atoms within one molecule (O and H) are held by strong polar COVALENT bonds — those are intramolecular. Hydrogen bonds form BETWEEN separate molecules, linking the δ+ hydrogen of one to the δ- oxygen of another. This is the single most common confusion in the topic, and mark schemes are strict about it: describing a hydrogen bond as being ‘inside’ the molecule loses the point.

faq.q: What is the difference between cohesion and adhesion?
faq.a: Both are hydrogen-bond attractions, but to different partners. COHESION is water sticking to water (same substance) — it holds the xylem water column together and creates surface tension. ADHESION is water sticking to a different polar surface, such as the cellulose walls of xylem — it helps water cling and rise. In transpiration the two work together: cohesion transmits the pull down an unbroken column, adhesion helps hold that column against the vessel walls.

faq.q: Why does water resist temperature change so well?
faq.a: When you heat water, the energy first goes into breaking hydrogen bonds rather than into speeding up the molecules, so the temperature climbs only slowly — this is its high specific heat capacity. It takes a lot of energy in, and gives a lot back out, before the temperature moves much. For life this means cell contents and large bodies of water stay thermally stable, and aquatic habitats do not swing wildly in temperature.

faq.q: If water is the ‘universal solvent’, why can't it dissolve fats?
faq.a: ‘Universal’ is an exaggeration — water dissolves POLAR and IONIC (hydrophilic) substances because it can wrap hydration shells around them. Fats and oils are non-polar (hydrophobic), so water molecules prefer to hydrogen-bond with each other rather than surround them. That failure to dissolve non-polar substances is itself essential: it lets hydrophobic phospholipid tails cluster away from water and form cell membranes.

faq.q: How does water's density behaviour help organisms in cold climates?
faq.a: Unlike most substances, water is LESS dense as a solid than as a liquid — the hydrogen bonds in ice hold molecules in an open lattice. So ice floats and forms an insulating layer on top of ponds and lakes, keeping the water beneath liquid. Fish and other organisms survive the winter below the ice, and the transparency of the water still lets light through for photosynthesis.

sections.content: Roughly two-thirds of your body is water, and life almost certainly began in it — yet water's importance is not an accident of abundance. It comes directly from the structure of a single small, bent molecule. Get that structure right and everything else in this topic falls out of it in a chain: polar bonds make the molecule polar, polarity makes hydrogen bonds, and hydrogen bonds make cohesion, surface tension, thermal buffering, evaporative cooling, solvent power and buoyancy — each of which maps onto a job water does inside living things. This lesson follows that chain link by link, and the model answer at the end is set out exactly the way our marking engine awards Paper 2 marks, because in an explain topic like this, WHICH distinct points you make decides how many marks you keep.
Polar covalent bonds and the bent shape

A water molecule is one oxygen atom joined to two hydrogen atoms by covalent bonds — pairs of shared electrons. But oxygen is far more electronegative than hydrogen, meaning it pulls the shared electrons more strongly towards itself. The electrons therefore spend more time near the oxygen, giving it a partial negative charge (written δ-) and leaving each hydrogen with a partial positive charge (δ+). Each O–H bond is said to be POLAR. Crucially, the molecule is not straight: the two O–H bonds meet at an angle of about 105°, so the molecule has a bent, wide-V shape. Because it is bent, the two bond dipoles do not point in opposite directions and cannot cancel — the oxygen end stays δ- and the hydrogen end stays δ+. The result is a molecule with an overall separation of charge: water is a POLAR molecule. (Contrast this with a symmetrical non-polar molecule such as methane, CH₄, where any small dipoles cancel and no overall charge separation remains.)

items: **Unequal sharing:** oxygen is more electronegative, so each O–H bond is polar — O is δ-, H is δ+.
**Bent shape:** the ~105° angle means the two bond dipoles do NOT cancel.
**Overall polar molecule:** water has a δ- (oxygen) end and a δ+ (hydrogen) end.
This permanent separation of charge is the origin of every property that follows.

sections.content: Hydrogen bonding between water molecules

Because each water molecule has a δ+ end and a δ- end, neighbouring molecules attract one another electrostatically: the δ+ hydrogen of one molecule is drawn to the δ- oxygen of another. This attraction is called a HYDROGEN BOND. It is an INTERMOLECULAR force — it acts between separate molecules — and it must not be confused with the far stronger covalent bonds WITHIN a molecule. A single hydrogen bond is weak, easily broken by thermal motion, and in liquid water hydrogen bonds are continually breaking and reforming. Their power lies entirely in their number: at any instant each water molecule can hydrogen-bond to up to four neighbours, so a sample of water is held together by a vast, self-healing network of these weak attractions. That collective grip is what gives rise to water's emergent properties — properties of the bulk liquid that no single molecule possesses on its own.

items: A hydrogen bond links the δ+ hydrogen of one molecule to the δ- oxygen of a neighbour.
Hydrogen bonds are INTERMOLECULAR (between molecules); covalent O–H bonds are intramolecular (within a molecule) and much stronger.
Each water molecule can form up to four hydrogen bonds.
Individually weak, but so numerous that collectively they dominate water's behaviour.
In liquid water they constantly break and reform.

sections.content: Cohesion, surface tension and adhesion

Cohesion is the attraction of water molecules to ONE ANOTHER through hydrogen bonding. It has two important consequences. First, it produces SURFACE TENSION: molecules at the surface are hydrogen-bonded only to neighbours below and beside them, so the surface behaves like a thin elastic skin — strong enough for pond skaters and other small insects to stand on. Second, cohesion holds a column of water together, so that a pull applied at the top is transmitted all the way down without the column snapping. ADHESION is different: it is the attraction of water molecules to OTHER polar surfaces, such as the cellulose of plant cell walls, again by hydrogen bonding. Adhesion lets water cling to and creep along such surfaces. In plants, cohesion and adhesion act together in the xylem: water evaporating from the leaves creates tension, cohesion transmits that pull down an unbroken column, and adhesion helps hold the column against the vessel walls, drawing water up against gravity.
Thermal properties: buffering and cooling

Two thermal properties both come from having to deal with hydrogen bonds. Water has a HIGH SPECIFIC HEAT CAPACITY: raising its temperature takes a large amount of energy, because incoming heat must first break hydrogen bonds before it can speed the molecules up. So water heats and cools slowly, buffering temperature — cell contents stay thermally stable, large bodies of water resist rapid temperature swings, and aquatic habitats remain more constant than the air above them. Water also has a HIGH LATENT HEAT OF VAPORISATION: turning liquid water into vapour requires a large amount of energy, because the many hydrogen bonds holding molecules in the liquid must be broken to release them as gas. This makes evaporation a powerful cooling mechanism — when water evaporates it carries away a great deal of heat. Mammals exploit this by SWEATING: as sweat evaporates from the skin, it removes heat and lowers body temperature. Plants do the same through TRANSPIRATION: evaporation of water from the leaves cools them, which matters on a hot, sunlit day.

items: **High specific heat capacity:** energy breaks hydrogen bonds before raising temperature → water heats/cools slowly → thermal buffering of cells and habitats.
**High latent heat of vaporisation:** evaporating water breaks many hydrogen bonds → a lot of heat is removed → efficient cooling.
**Biological cooling:** sweating in animals and transpiration in plants both use evaporative heat loss.

sections.content: Water as a near-universal solvent

Because water is polar, it interacts strongly with other charged and polar particles. Around a positive ion the δ- oxygen ends of water molecules cluster; around a negative ion the δ+ hydrogen ends cluster. These HYDRATION SHELLS pull the particles apart and keep them in solution, so water dissolves ionic compounds (such as sodium chloride) and polar molecules (such as glucose and amino acids). Substances that dissolve in or interact readily with water are called HYDROPHILIC (‘water-loving’) — they are polar or charged. Substances that do not dissolve, such as fats and oils, are HYDROPHOBIC (‘water-fearing’) — they are non-polar, and water molecules prefer to hydrogen-bond with each other rather than surround them. Water's solvent power makes it the transport medium of life: blood plasma carries dissolved glucose, ions, amino acids and wastes, and xylem and phloem carry dissolved minerals and sugars. Equally, water's inability to dissolve hydrophobic molecules is essential — it drives phospholipid tails together to form the cell membranes that compartmentalise every cell.
Buoyancy and transparency

Two further properties shape life in water. Unusually, water is LESS dense as a solid than as a liquid: in ice the hydrogen bonds lock molecules into an open lattice that holds them further apart than in the liquid. So ice floats, forming an insulating layer that keeps the water beneath from freezing — aquatic organisms survive the winter below it, and floating ice also provides buoyant support and a surface for some animals. Liquid water is also TRANSPARENT, allowing light to penetrate to a useful depth. This lets aquatic plants, algae and other photosynthesisers capture light below the surface, supporting the food chains of ponds, lakes and the sunlit upper ocean.

sections.question: Explain why sweating is an effective way for a mammal to cool down, and why this depends on a property of water. [3]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
Water has a high latent heat of vaporisation, because a large amount of energy is needed to break the many hydrogen bonds holding water molecules in the liquid before they can escape as vapour. When sweat evaporates from the skin, this energy is taken from the body as heat. Removing that heat lowers the body's temperature, cooling the mammal.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 3 marks** — each distinct valid point scores 1:

- **Point 1 (M):** water has a high latent heat of vaporisation / a lot of energy is needed to evaporate it, because hydrogen bonds must be broken. [reasoning]
- **Point 2 (M):** as sweat evaporates it takes/removes heat energy FROM the body. [reasoning]
- **Point 3 (A):** this lowers body temperature / cools the organism. [the correct link/conclusion]

*A bare statement such as ‘sweat cools you down’ with no mention of evaporation removing heat, or no link to a water property, does not reach the mechanism marks. Naming the property (high latent heat of vaporisation) and the transfer of heat away from the body is what earns the credit.*

sections.question: The specific heat capacity of water is 4.2 J g⁻¹ °C⁻¹. A 60 kg swimmer's body and a small 30 kg pool of water each absorb 1.0 × 10⁶ J of heat energy from the Sun. Estimate the temperature rise of the pool water, and explain what this illustrates about water as a habitat. [4]
sections.solution:
**Step 1 — choose the relationship.**
Heat energy $Q = m\,c\,\Delta T$, so $\Delta T = \dfrac{Q}{m\,c}$.  [M1: correct rearrangement]

**Step 2 — put in the values for the pool (in grams to match the units of $c$).**
$m = 30\text{ kg} = 30\,000$ g, $c = 4.2$ J g⁻¹ °C⁻¹, $Q = 1.0 \times 10^{6}$ J.
$\Delta T = \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{6}}{30\,000 \times 4.2} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{6}}{1.26 \times 10^{5}} \approx 7.9$ °C.  [M1: correct substitution; A1: ≈ 7.9 °C]

**Step 3 — interpret biologically.**
Even this fairly large heat input raises the water temperature by only about 8 °C, and a larger natural body of water would rise far less. Because water has a high specific heat capacity, aquatic habitats change temperature slowly and stay thermally stable, so organisms living in them are buffered against rapid temperature swings. [A1: links high specific heat capacity to thermal stability of the habitat]

*Note the unit care: mass must be in grams to match $c$ in J g⁻¹ °C⁻¹. Under error-carried-forward, a correct method with a slipped unit conversion still earns the method marks.*

sections.content: In every ‘explain a property of water’ question, one-word properties are not enough. Build the full chain: structure (polar bonds → bent shape → polarity → hydrogen bonds) → the named property → the biological role. For example: ‘Water is polar and forms hydrogen bonds (1); this gives strong cohesion (1) that holds the water column together so it can be pulled up the xylem (1).’ Each link in that chain is a separate mark.
Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Putting hydrogen bonds inside the molecule** — hydrogen bonds are INTERMOLECULAR, between the δ+ H of one molecule and the δ- O of another. The O–H bonds within a molecule are covalent. Describing a hydrogen bond as being ‘within’ a water molecule scores nothing.
**Calling hydrogen bonds strong bonds** — a single hydrogen bond is WEAK; water's properties come from their large NUMBER acting together, not from any one being strong.
**Confusing cohesion and adhesion** — cohesion is water-to-water (same substance); adhesion is water-to-a-different-surface. Swapping them in a transpiration answer loses the mark.
**Saying water has a ‘high boiling point’ as the explanation** without mentioning hydrogen bonds — the property must be traced back to the energy needed to break hydrogen bonds.
**Muddling hydrophilic and hydrophobic** — hydrophilic = polar/charged and dissolves; hydrophobic = non-polar and does not. Calling a lipid hydrophilic reverses the biology.

**Confusing specific heat capacity with latent heat of vaporisation** — specific heat capacity is about raising temperature (buffering); latent heat of vaporisation is about evaporating (cooling). They are different properties with different biological roles.
**Writing ‘water is important for life’ or ‘water is essential’ with no mechanism** — a property with no structural cause and no biological use earns zero. Always give structure → property → role.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

A1.1 explain questions are marked ANALYTICALLY, not against a level or markband: each distinct valid biological point is worth one mark. Reasoning marks (M) credit a correct piece of mechanism (for example polarity → hydrogen bonding), answer marks (A) credit the correct LINK to a biological role, and error-carried-forward (ECF) means that if you build correctly on an earlier idea you keep the later marks even if a detail slips. Equivalent wording is accepted — you do not have to use the exact phrases below — but the engine rejects vague claims with no mechanism. Study how each mark is tied to a specific named idea.

sections.question: Explain how the polarity of water molecules gives rise to TWO properties that are important for living organisms. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
Because oxygen is more electronegative and the molecule is bent, a water molecule is polar, with a δ- oxygen end and a δ+ hydrogen end. This polarity lets the δ+ hydrogen of one molecule attract the δ- oxygen of another, forming hydrogen bonds between the molecules. These hydrogen bonds make water COHESIVE — the molecules stick together — which holds an unbroken column of water in the xylem so that water can be pulled up to the leaves of a plant. The same polarity also makes water a good SOLVENT: it forms hydration shells around ions and other polar (hydrophilic) substances, dissolving them so that blood plasma can transport glucose, ions and amino acids around the body.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks** — each distinct valid point scores 1 (maximum 4):

- **Point 1 (M):** water is polar / has δ- oxygen and δ+ hydrogen ends (from unequal electron sharing and the bent shape). This is the required starting mechanism.
- **Point 2 (M):** polarity allows HYDROGEN BONDS to form between water molecules (δ+ H of one to δ- O of another).
- **Point 3 (A):** a first named property + its biological use — e.g. COHESION → water column held together / pulled up the xylem to the leaves. Property and use together earn the link mark.
- **Point 4 (A):** a SECOND, DIFFERENT named property + its biological use — e.g. SOLVENT → dissolves glucose/ions/amino acids so blood plasma can transport them. (Other valid pairs: high specific heat capacity → thermal buffering; high latent heat of vaporisation → cooling by sweating/transpiration; adhesion → water clings to xylem walls.)

Because more than four creditable points exist, the engine caps the award at 4 — there is room to miss one and still score full marks. But the marks are for DISTINCT ideas: giving cohesion twice in different words scores once, and you must name TWO different properties, each tied to a biological use.

**ECF and equivalents.** The points are independent. A student who states polarity and hydrogen bonding, then correctly links only one property, keeps those points and simply misses the fourth. Equivalent phrasing is accepted (‘sticks to itself’ for cohesion, ‘dissolves polar substances’ for solvent).

**What the engine rejects.** An answer that says ‘water is polar so it is important for life’ or ‘water is essential for organisms’ scores ZERO beyond any polarity point, because it names no mechanism and no specific property-plus-use. The engine credits structure → property → role, not a bare importance claim.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR answer this same way — point by point, with ECF and a cap at the marks available. So write in distinct, creditable statements: name the polarity, name the hydrogen bonding, then give TWO different properties each with a real biological use. Vague ‘water is important for life’ lines earn nothing; naming the mechanism and showing each link is what protects your score.
Where this leads

Every idea here reappears across the course. Hydrogen bonding returns in the pairing of DNA bases and in the folding of proteins; water as a solvent underlies enzyme reactions, blood transport and membrane structure; and evaporative cooling connects to thermoregulation and to transport in plants. Hold onto the single habit this lesson teaches — trace each property back to the polar, hydrogen-bonded molecule, and forward to a biological role — and much of the chemistry of life becomes a story about one small bent molecule.
